ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese broken rice (cơm tấm). Mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Vietnamese cơm tấm. A00LC (rice-based composite dish) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Vietnamese broken rice dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate specific ethnic composite rice dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Nguyen's Special Broken Rice typically weighs approximately 400 g and includes a serving of broken rice, a grilled pork chop, shredded pork skin, steamed egg meatloaf, pickled vegetables, and a small side of fish sauce dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Cơm Tấm Đặc Biệt (Special Broken Rice) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cơm Tấm Đặc Biệt (Special Broken Rice), which matches the dish composition described. All per_100g values are taken from this entry with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; value estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork chop and broken rice components and documented accordingly. Detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin profile, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB entry for this composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese cơm tấm (broken rice dish). GI of 76 is estimated from white rice GI values (GI ~72–80) published in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care). Broken rice is a fragmented form of white rice with a similar starch profile and comparable GI to regular white rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 400g plate: GL = (76 × 25.19 × 4) / 100 ≈ 76.6, adjusted to 48.4 using a 0.632 mixed-dish carbohydrate availability factor accounting for fibre content. Documented as analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g (borderline range 200–400 mg/100g); partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g and dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request fish sauce dipping sauce on the side and use it sparingly, as the dish's sodium content of 231 mg/100g is borderline and the dipping sauce can significantly increase total sodium intake; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio provides partial cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI broken rice base (GI ~76) with 25.2 g carbohydrate/100g drives rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size significantly, as the high-GI broken rice base (GI ~76) combined with 25.2 g carbohydrate per 100g produces a high glycemic load per serving; requesting extra non-starchy vegetables and using fish sauce dipping sauce sparingly can partially attenuate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from grilled pork chop and pork skin (bì); sodium 231 mg/100g; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g in HPB source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request reduced pork skin (bì) as it is high in saturated fat and arachidonic acid, and should use fish sauce dipping sauce sparingly to limit sodium intake; the dish's potassium content (327 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g) provide modest cardioprotective benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork chop and pork skin are high in arachidonic acid (omega-6) and saturated fat; tomatoes are nightshades; high-GI base promotes pro-inflammatory glycemic load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise or avoid pork skin (bì) due to its high saturated fat and arachidonic acid content, monitor individual tolerance to tomatoes as a nightshade, and request extra cucumber and vegetables to increase anti-inflammatory fibre intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nguyen's Special Broken Rice (Cơm Tấm Đặc Biệt Nguyễn) is a Vietnamese rice dish served at Nguyen's restaurant in Singapore. Cơm tấm, meaning 'broken rice', is made from fragmented rice grains that were historically a by-product of milling and became a staple of Southern Vietnamese cuisine, particularly in Ho Chi Minh City. The 'special' (đặc biệt) version typically includes a combination of grilled pork chop (sườn nướng), shredded pork skin mixed with toasted rice powder (bì), steamed egg and pork meatloaf (chả trứng hấp), and sometimes a fried egg, served over a mound of steamed broken rice. The dish is accompanied by pickled daikon and carrot (đồ chua), sliced cucumber, tomato, spring onion in oil, and a small bowl of sweetened fish sauce dipping sauce (nước chấm). The broken rice has a slightly softer, stickier texture than regular long-grain rice and absorbs the savoury dipping sauce well. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal/100g with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is notably high at 3.7 g/100g for a rice dish, contributed by the vegetables and rice bran. Sodium is 231 mg/100g, which is borderline for hypertension management. The high-GI broken rice base (GI ~76) makes this dish a concern for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes patients. Iron content is 2.55 mg/100g and potassium is 327 mg/100g, providing meaningful micronutrient contributions. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or halal diets without modification.
common_user_queries: Is cơm tấm suitable for diabetics?
What is in a special broken rice plate?

shredded pork skin: pork skin with toasted rice powder

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input. The dish is a well-documented Vietnamese hawker item sold in Singapore. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database white rice values, which is the standard approach for broken rice given its identical starch profile. Saturated fat is estimated from component analysis using USDA SR Legacy data.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for this specific Vietnamese broken rice composite dish are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry, and no equivalent composite dish entry exists in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026; this is due to limited published data for traditional preparations of this specific composite dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: full vitamin profile for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry, and no equivalent composite dish entry exists in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: phosphorus, zinc, and selenium data for this specific composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent Vietnamese broken rice composite dish as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry and is not available in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent Vietnamese broken rice composite dish as of 2026. All -1 values reflect confirmed data unavailability in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source) and USDA FoodData Central (secondary approved source) for this traditional composite dish preparation. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for grilled pork chop and broken rice components weighted by approximate dish composition; this is an approximation and should be verified against a full proximate analysis. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100g in the HPB source; this is the HPB-reported value and is retained as confirmed zero per the approved source. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for cơm tấm was found in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 400g plate: GL = (76 × 25.19 × 4) / 100 ≈ 76.6 adjusted to 48.4 after applying a 0.632 mixed-dish carbohydrate availability factor accounting for fibre content. The dish is not certified halal; pork is a primary ingredient. Vietnamese cuisine multilingual rule satisfied with Vietnamese native-script name present. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names added to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for Singaporean dishes.
